content:
# Acts Chapter 25 -- The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF pages 256-257 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_025_page_001.jpg, ACT_025_page_002.jpg

---

**1.** Festus therefore, having arrived-into the province, after three days went-up to Jerusalem from Caesarea.

**2.** And the chief-priests and the leading-men of the Jews made-clear [charges] to him against Paul, and were-urging him,

**3.** asking a favor against him, so-that he-might-send-for him to Jerusalem, making-ready an ambush to kill him along the way.

**4.** Then Festus answered [that] Paul is-being-kept in Caesarea, and that he-himself is-about to go-out in-quickness.

**5.** "Those therefore among you," he-says, "the ones-having-power, having-gone-down-together, if there-is anything wrong in the man, let-them-accuse him."

**6.** And having-spent among them not more than eight or ten days, having-gone-down to Caesarea, on-the-next-day having-sat upon the judgment-seat, he-commanded Paul to-be-brought.

**7.** And when-he-arrived, the Jews who had-come-down from Jerusalem stood-around [him], bringing many and heavy charges which they-were not able to-prove,

**8.** Paul making-defense [that]: "Neither against the law of-the-Jews, nor against the temple, nor against Caesar have-I sinned [in]-anything."

**9.** But Festus, wishing to-lay-up a favor with-the-Jews, answering to-Paul said: "Are-you-willing, having-gone-up to Jerusalem, there to-be-judged concerning these-things before me?"

**10.** But Paul said: "Upon the judgment-seat of-Caesar I-am-standing, where it-is-necessary [for] me to-be-judged. [Against] the Jews I-have-done-wrong [in]-nothing, as also you very-well know.

**11.** If therefore I-am-doing-wrong and have-done anything worthy of-death, I-do not refuse to-die; but if none of-the-things [is true] of-which these-ones accuse me, no-one is-able to-hand me-over to-them. I-appeal-to Caesar."

**12.** Then Festus, having-spoken-with the council, answered: "To-Caesar you-have-appealed; to Caesar you-shall-go."

**13.** And days having-passed, some, Agrippa the king and Bernice arrived at Caesarea, having-greeted Festus.

**14.** And as they-were-spending more days there, Festus laid-before the king the-things concerning Paul, saying: "A-certain man is left-behind [as] a-prisoner by Felix,

**15.** concerning whom, when-I-was in Jerusalem, the chief-priests and the elders of-the-Jews made-clear [charges], asking condemnation against him.

**16.** To whom I-answered that it-is not a-custom for-Romans to-hand-over any man before the-one-being-accused might-have the accusers face to face, and might-receive a-place of-defense concerning the charge.

**17.** Therefore when-they-had-come-together here, making no delay, on-the-next day having-sat upon the judgment-seat, I-commanded the man to-be-brought.

**18.** Concerning whom, having-stood, the accusers were-bringing no charge of-evil-things which I was-supposing,

**19.** but certain questions concerning their-own religion they-had against him, and concerning a-certain Jesus having-died, whom Paul was-asserting to-be-alive.

**20.** And I, being-at-a-loss concerning the inquiry into these-things, was-saying whether he-might-wish to-go to Jerusalem and there to-be-judged concerning these-things.

**21.** But Paul having-appealed to-be-kept for the decision of-the Augustus, I-commanded him to-be-kept until I-might-send him up to Caesar."

**22.** And Agrippa [said] to Festus: "I-was-wishing also myself to-hear the man." "Tomorrow," he-says, "you-shall-hear him."

**23.** On-the-next-day therefore, Agrippa having-come and Bernice with much display, and having-entered into the audience-hall, with both commanders and men of-prominence of-the-city, and Festus having-commanded, Paul was-brought.

**24.** And Festus says: "King Agrippa, and all the men having-been-present-with us, you-see this-one concerning whom all the multitude of-the-Jews petitioned me, both in Jerusalem and here, crying-out [that] he-ought not to-live any-longer.

**25.** But I myself perceived [that] he-had-done nothing worthy of-death, and this-one himself having-appealed to the Augustus, I-decided to-send [him].

**26.** Concerning whom anything certain to-write to-the-lord I-do not have. Therefore I-brought him-forth before you-all, and especially before you, King Agrippa, so-that the examination having-taken-place, I-might-have something to-write.

**27.** For unreasonable it-seems to-me, sending a-prisoner, not also the charges against him to-signify."

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

### Manuscript Observations (Codex Sinaiticus, 4th century)

1. **Verse 6 -- "eight or ten days"**: Sinaiticus reads ου πλειους ημερας οκτω η δεκα (not more than eight or ten days). Some later manuscripts read "not more than ten days" omitting the "eight or." The Sinaiticus reading preserves the deliberative imprecision of the original narrator.

2. **Verse 13 -- ασπασαμενοι (having-greeted)**: Sinaiticus uses the aorist middle participle, indicating that Agrippa and Bernice came to Caesarea to pay respects to the new governor. Some manuscripts read ασπασομενοι (future participle, "intending to greet"), which alters the temporal relationship. Sinaiticus preserves the completed action.

3. **Verse 16 -- "it-is not a-custom for-Romans"**: The Greek reads ουκ εστιν εθος Ρωμαιοις. I have rendered this with wooden literalness. The accusative-infinitive construction following ("to-hand-over any man") preserves the legal register of Festus's speech.

4. **Verse 19 -- δεισιδαιμονιας (religion)**: This word carries ambiguity -- it can mean "religion" (neutral) or "superstition" (pejorative). Given that Festus is speaking diplomatically to King Agrippa, "religion" is the contextually appropriate rendering, but the reader should be aware of the semantic range. I render it neutrally as Festus would before a Jewish king.

5. **Verse 21 -- του Σεβαστου (the Augustus)**: Literally "the Revered One." This is the Greek translation of the Latin title "Augustus," used as a formal title for the Roman Emperor (at this time Nero). I have transliterated it as "the Augustus" to preserve its titular function rather than rendering it as a common adjective.

6. **Verse 25 -- Repetition of Σεβαστος**: Sinaiticus uses τον Σεβαστον again rather than Καισαρα, maintaining the formal imperial title consistently in Festus's speech to Agrippa.

7. **Verse 26 -- τω κυριω (to-the-lord)**: Festus uses κυριος for the Emperor, a politically charged term. This is the same word used of God and of Christ elsewhere in Acts. The political-theological tension is deliberate in Luke's narrative and must not be flattened in translation.

8. **Verse 22 -- Imperfect εβουλομην (I-was-wishing)**: The imperfect tense here conveys a nuance of unfulfilled desire or polite expression -- "I was wishing" (i.e., "I have been wanting"). This is not a simple past tense but an idiomatic use of the imperfect for courteous request. I preserve the tense literally.

9. **Verse 9 -- χαριν καταθεσθαι (to-lay-up a favor)**: Sinaiticus reads χαριν καταθεσθαι τοις Ιουδαιοις. This phrase echoes the identical construction used of Felix in Acts 24:27, creating a deliberate literary parallel between the two governors' political motivations.

10. **General Note on Sinaiticus readings**: The text of Acts 25 in Sinaiticus is generally well-preserved and closely aligns with the Alexandrian text-type. No major lacunae or corrections by later hands are visible in this portion. The scriptio continua (continuous writing without word division) is consistent throughout.

-- EMET
